_meta.name: GoodMedika
_meta.description: Desktop port of ghost_shell_browser/scripts/smart-search-and-click.json. Same brand-SERP rotation logic, retargeted at the desktop runner's primitive set: 'loop' becomes 'foreach' (with 'var'/'body' instead of 'item_var'/'steps'), variable interpolation uses '{{item}}' instead of '{item}', and the web's compound 'commercial_inflate' / 'search_query' actions are emulated with 'navigate' + 'wait_for_selector' + 'pause' (the desktop runner doesn't have those compound actions yet — Selenium's GoToUrl auto-percent-encodes the Cyrillic query so plain navigate is enough). Behaviour matches: each iteration optionally pre-warms Google with a generic commercial query (~30 % chance), runs the brand search, walks every ad on the SERP, and clicks any ad whose domain isn't on the profile's my_domains list.

name: GoodMedika
description: Brand SERP rotation with optional commercial pre-warm. Click any non-own ad (competitors + targets). For target-only behaviour set only_on_target=true on click_ad — the ad_is_external outer condition is permissive enough to let it filter inside.

steps._comment: Top-level loop over the brand keyword list. The desktop runner's `foreach` mirrors the web's `loop`: it iterates each line of `items`, exposes the current value as ctx.Vars[var], and runs `body` per iteration. shuffle/random ordering is NOT yet supported on desktop foreach — items run top-to-bottom. If you need shuffling, duplicate the file with the brand list pre-shuffled.

body._comment: Commercial pre-warm — emulates the web's `commercial_inflate` step. probability=0.3 means ~30 % of iterations get the warm-up. We navigate to a generic commercial query so Google's commercial-intent context absorbs the brand-adjacent signal; the next brand search lands in a richer ad context. wait_for_selector pauses until the SERP is rendered (#search), then a short dwell mimics scan time before we move on.

Real brand search. Selenium auto-percent-encodes the query, so the Cyrillic spellings work without manual escaping. The desktop runner has no built-in retry-on-no-ads loop (the web's `search_query` wraps four attempts with 13–15 s gaps); foreach_ad handles the empty-SERP case by simply iterating zero items.

Walk every ad on the SERP. foreach_ad auto-calls parse_ads once per entry if the ad list is empty, so no explicit parse_ads step is needed before this. Inside the body, the if(ad_is_external) gate filters to non-own ads (covers competitors AND target-domain ads). To click ONLY pure competitors, switch the condition kind to ad_is_competitor.

ad_is_external = 'any ad NOT on my domain' (covers competitors AND target-domain ads). Use this when you want flexibility inside via only_on_target / skip_on_target. To click ONLY pure competitors (excluding targets), switch kind to ad_is_competitor.

then._comment: skip_on_my_domain stays as a paranoid safety net (the outer ad_is_external already excludes own domain — but if my_domains is misconfigured at run-time, this is the second line of defence). only_on_target=false → click both competitors and targets. Flip to true if you want target-only. The desktop's click_ad uses a 2.5–6 s post-click dwell internally; dwell_min/dwell_max from the web schema are accepted as params but are no-ops for now (kept for forward-compat).
Short between-click pause inside foreach_ad. probability=0.6 means most of the time we pause — but not always, so the click cadence varies.

body._comment: Cool-down between query-loop iterations. Real users don't fire searches back-to-back without breathing room.
